Category: --- ACTIVITY STRUCTURE: how work is represented in the schedule ---

Description: The lowest-level schedulable work item — has its own duration, logic ties, resources, and cost assignment. The fundamental unit of CPM scheduling. Typical duration 5-20 working days; activities exceeding 20 days indicate insufficient detail. Includes Level of Effort (LOE) for time-dependent items (supervision, general conditions, temporary facilities) that span a period regardless of work accomplished.
Common Values: 5-20 working day duration (typical); should not exceed 2 update periods; LOE for supervision/general conditions
Source Systems: P6; MS Project; Asta Powerproject; Procore; Tilos
Procore Coverage: High — scheduling activity tables with duration, dates, progress, and resource fields
Benchmarking Use: Activity count and average duration are key schedule quality metrics
Notes: Activities > 40 working days are a red flag in most specifications. LOE activities should not be on the critical path.

Description: Roll-up activity that aggregates child activities into a single bar — no independent duration or resources. Used for Work Breakdown Structure nodes, phase summaries, and area roll-ups. Hammock activities span between two other activities and recalculate automatically. Summary and WBS nodes organize the schedule hierarchy for filtering, reporting, and executive communication.
Common Values: WBS summary, phase summary, area summary, hammock (duration-driven span)
Source Systems: P6; MS Project; Asta Powerproject; Procore
Procore Coverage: Medium — imported from CPM tool; WBS structure visible but hierarchy may flatten on import
Benchmarking Use: Activity count by level indicates schedule hierarchy depth and organization quality
Notes: Summary activities should not have logic ties in most specifications. Hammocks are useful for measuring elapsed time of a phase.

Description: Zero-duration marker representing a key event — start milestone or finish milestone. Contractual milestones carry liquidated damages. Interim milestones (dry-in, permanent power, certificate of occupancy) are schedule gates that trigger downstream work. The primary communication tool between owner and contractor for schedule status.
Common Values: Start milestones, finish milestones, contractual milestones, interim milestones, procurement milestones
Source Systems: P6; MS Project; Procore
Procore Coverage: High — milestones identified by zero duration in schedule tables; calendar items also capture milestones
Benchmarking Use: Milestone adherence rate = milestones hit on time / milestones due
Notes: Contractual milestones with liquidated damages are the highest-consequence schedule elements.

Description: The naming and coding conventions that make activities findable, filterable, and analyzable. Activity ID follows a coding convention encoding phase/area/trade (e.g., A1000, 03-EL-0100). Activity Name should describe scope, location, and trade (e.g., 'Install Ductwork - Floor 3 Zone A'). Consistent identification enables automated analysis and NLP-based activity classification across projects.
Common Values: ID format varies by contractor; Name format: [Action] [Element] [Location]
Source Systems: P6; MS Project; Procore
Procore Coverage: High — activity ID and name imported from source tool; custom fields support additional coding
Benchmarking Use: Name consistency enables NLP-based activity classification for cross-project comparison
Notes: Good IDs encode WBS level, trade, and sequence. Good names include what work, where, by whom. Quality varies enormously across contractors.

Description: The time dimension of an activity — original duration (planned at baseline), remaining duration (days left from data date), actual start/finish dates, and percent complete. Duration types: Fixed Duration (most common — adding crew doesn't proportionally reduce time), Fixed Units (resource-driven), Physical % Complete (quantity-based — most reliable for earned value). Progress tracking method determines earned value accuracy.
Common Values: Original 5-20 working days; Remaining = Original x (1-% Complete); Physical % preferred over duration %
Source Systems: P6; MS Project; Procore; field measurement systems
Procore Coverage: High — duration, actual dates, and percent complete fields in scheduling tables; daily productivity logs validate physical progress
Benchmarking Use: Average duration is a schedule quality metric; remaining duration inflation signals trouble
Notes: Remaining duration creep (remaining > original) is a leading indicator of schedule slip. Physical % from field measurement is most accurate.

Description: Classification codes assigned to activities for grouping, filtering, sorting, and reporting — the dimensional analysis framework for schedule data. Common codes: Trade (electrical, mechanical), Area/Zone (Building A Floor 3), Phase (foundations, structure), Responsibility (GC, sub name), CSI Division. Code completeness determines what analytics are possible.
Common Values: Trade, area/zone, phase, responsibility, CSI division, building system, crew
Source Systems: P6 (Activity Codes); MS Project (custom fields); Procore (custom fields on activities)
Procore Coverage: Medium — custom field framework supports activity codes but adoption depends on contractor setup
Benchmarking Use: Code completeness enables dimensional analysis; consistent trade codes allow cross-project comparison
Notes: Custom field framework replaces rigid activity code structures with extensible fields. Enables trade, area, phase, and responsibility analysis.

Category: --- LOGIC & DEPENDENCIES: how activities connect and drive the schedule ---

Description: The logical connection between two activities that drives the CPM calculation. Four types: Finish-to-Start (FS, most common, ~80-90% of ties), Start-to-Start (SS, overlapping work), Finish-to-Finish (FF, activities ending together), Start-to-Finish (SF, rarely used, often error). SS+FF pairs represent parallel work sequences. Relationship type ratios are a core schedule quality metric — specifications often require >80% FS.
Common Values: FS (~80-90% of ties), SS (often paired with FF), FF (paired with SS), SF (<1% — often error)
Source Systems: P6; MS Project; Procore
Procore Coverage: High — scheduling relationship tables capture typed dependencies with lag values
Benchmarking Use: FS ratio is a schedule quality metric; specs often require >80% FS
Notes: Heavy SS/FF without pairing can obscure the critical path. SF ties should be flagged for review. DCMA 14-point check #4.

Description: Time offset on a logical relationship — positive lag represents waiting time (concrete cure 7-28 days, submittal review 14-21 days, coating dry 1-3 days). Negative lag (lead) represents overlap — many specifications prohibit entirely. Lag should represent real waiting time with documented justification. Excessive lag can hide float and distort the critical path. Lag used to manipulate float is a common schedule gaming tactic.
Common Values: Concrete cure 7-28 days; submittal review 14-21 days; coating dry 1-3 days; negative lag prohibited by many specs
Source Systems: P6; MS Project; Procore
Procore Coverage: High — lag values captured on relationships with display units
Benchmarking Use: Lag ratio (activities with lag / total) indicates potential schedule manipulation
Notes: Best practice: use SS relationships instead of negative lag on FS ties. Lag >20 days should document what's being waited for. DCMA checks #2 and #3.

Description: Categorization of why two activities are linked — determines which relationships can change during delay analysis. Mandatory (hard logic): physical or contractual requirement (foundation before structure). Discretionary (soft logic): preferred sequence based on practice or resources (east wing before west). External: driven by events outside the team's control (permits, utility connections, owner-furnished items).
Common Values: Mandatory (physical sequence), Discretionary (preferred order), External (third-party driven)
Source Systems: P6 (convention only — not tagged); MS Project; scheduling methodology
Procore Coverage: Low — dependency classification not explicitly modeled; requires scheduler judgment and documentation
Benchmarking Use: Soft logic changes in delay analysis reveal schedule flexibility
Notes: P6 and MS Project don't natively distinguish hard from soft logic. This classification exists in scheduler judgment and delay analysis documentation.

Description: Activities missing predecessors (open start) or successors (open finish) — logic gaps that distort CPM calculations. Only the project start and finish milestones should have open ends. Open starts can begin on the data date regardless of logic. Open finishes don't drive anything downstream. Both cause unreliable float and critical path results.
Common Values: Open starts = missing predecessor; Open finishes = missing successor; Target: 0 except start/finish milestones
Source Systems: P6 (Schedule Check); MS Project; Procore (calculable from relationship data)
Procore Coverage: Medium — calculable from scheduling relationship data but requires analysis logic
Benchmarking Use: Open end count is a critical schedule quality metric; DCMA requires zero open ends
Notes: The most fundamental schedule quality issue. One of the DCMA 14-point checks. Open ends invalidate float calculations.

Description: Schedule connectedness measured by relationship-to-activity ratio. Logic density healthy range: 1.5-2.5 relationships per activity. Below 1.2 means loosely connected (CPM unreliable). Above 3.0 may indicate over-constraining. Redundant logic: relationships that don't affect calculations because another path already controls dates — not harmful but adds noise and reduces readability.
Common Values: Density 1.5-2.5 (healthy); <1.2 (poor logic); >3.0 (over-connected); Redundant ties = housekeeping issue
Source Systems: P6 (Schedule Check); MS Project; Procore (calculable from relationship and activity data)
Procore Coverage: Medium — calculable from scheduling relationship and activity data
Benchmarking Use: Logic density is a core schedule quality metric for cross-project comparison
Notes: Low density means CPM calculation is unreliable — activities float freely without proper logic connections.

Category: --- TIME MANAGEMENT: calendars, constraints, and date types ---

Description: Definition of working days and hours that drives duration-to-date conversion in the CPM. Standard 5-day (Mon-Fri, default), 6-day (adds Saturday, +17% capacity), 7-day (continuous — for cure time and critical path compression), weather-adjusted (anticipated non-work days by season/region), and holiday calendars. Calendar assignment must match activity type — cure time on a 5-day calendar adds phantom non-work weekends.
Common Values: 5-day (default), 6-day (compressed), 7-day (cure/continuous), weather (regional), holiday (contractual)
Source Systems: P6 (Global/Project/Resource calendars); MS Project (Base calendar); Procore
Procore Coverage: Medium — calendar name available on scheduling activities but full calendar definitions not imported
Benchmarking Use: Calendar mix (5/6/7-day usage ratio) indicates schedule pressure and acceleration level across projects
Notes: Concrete cure on a 5-day calendar is a common error. Weather calendar thresholds per month define excusable delay for time extensions.

Description: Date override on an activity that restricts CPM calculation — from permissive (ASAP, the default) to absolute (Must Start/Finish On). Every non-default constraint should have documented justification. High constraint ratios indicate date-driven rather than logic-driven scheduling, producing unreliable float and critical path results.
Common Values: ASAP (default), SNET (start no earlier than), SNLT (start no later than), FNLT (finish no later than), Must Start/Finish On (<1%)
Source Systems: P6; MS Project; Procore
Procore Coverage: Medium — constraint type and date available on scheduling activities
Benchmarking Use: Constraint ratio (non-ASAP / total) — target <5%; >10% concerning; >20% alarm
Notes: Must constraints break CPM logic entirely. FNLT on the last activity typically sets the contractual completion date. Negative float against FNLT = projected late.

Description: Dates produced by the CPM forward and backward pass calculations. Early Start/Early Finish: earliest possible dates from forward pass through logic and calendars. Late Start/Late Finish: latest dates from backward pass without delaying the project. The difference (LS-ES or LF-EF) is Total Float. These are calculated outputs that respond to logic, durations, calendars, and constraints — not manual inputs.
Common Values: Early Start (ES), Early Finish (EF), Late Start (LS), Late Finish (LF); Total Float = LS-ES = LF-EF
Source Systems: P6; MS Project; Procore (imported with scheduled dates)
Procore Coverage: Medium — early/late dates imported from CPM tool; some loss of granularity on import
Benchmarking Use: Planned dates anchor all schedule performance measurement
Notes: Forward pass: ES = max(predecessor EF + lag). Backward pass: LF = min(successor LS - lag). Critical path = where ES = LS.

Description: The status date of the schedule update — divides past from future. All progress is recorded as-of the data date. Activities with actual start after the data date or remaining work before it are out-of-sequence. Typically month-end or contractual cut-off. Data date advancement is the heartbeat of schedule management — each monthly update advances it and recalculates the network.
Common Values: Month-end or contractual cut-off; advances with each update cycle; monthly standard, bi-weekly on fast-track
Source Systems: P6 (Data Date); MS Project (Status Date); Procore (imported with schedule metadata)
Procore Coverage: Medium — imported data date from schedule metadata; distinguishes current from superseded
Benchmarking Use: Data date consistency enables period-over-period schedule comparison
Notes: Data date currency (how recently updated) is itself a schedule management quality signal. Stale data dates indicate neglected schedules.

Description: The approved planned dates from the baseline schedule — the 'as-planned' reference against which all performance is measured. Original baseline (BL0) is the contractual reference submitted 30-90 days after NTP. Re-baselines (BL1, BL2) reflect approved scope changes. All variance analysis references a specific baseline. Baseline dates are immutable once approved.
Common Values: BL0 = original plan, BL1/BL2 = re-baselines after approved changes; variance = actual minus baseline
Source Systems: P6 (Maintain Baselines); MS Project (Set Baseline); Procore (baseline via parent schedule linkage)
Procore Coverage: Medium — baseline linkage via schedule metadata; parent schedule connects to baselines
Benchmarking Use: Actual vs. baseline variance is the primary schedule performance metric
Notes: BL0 is sacrosanct — modifications require formal owner approval. Too-frequent re-baselining obscures true performance.

Category: --- SCHEDULE ANALYSIS: float, critical path, and performance measurement ---

Description: Calendar days an activity can be delayed without downstream or project-level impact. Total Float: delay without delaying project completion (LF-EF). Free Float: delay without delaying any immediate successor. Negative Float: the schedule exceeds the completion date — the project is projected late. Float distribution (histogram of TF values across all activities) is a key schedule health indicator.
Common Values: Critical: TF=0; Near-critical: TF 1-10 days; Healthy: TF >20 days; Negative float = projected late finish
Source Systems: P6; MS Project; Procore
Procore Coverage: High — total_float field on scheduling activities; is_critical flag
Benchmarking Use: Float distribution histogram and negative float magnitude indicate schedule health
Notes: Artificial constraints can inflate or deflate float. Negative float triggers recovery schedule requirement in most contracts. DCMA checks #6, #7.

Description: The longest continuous chain of activities and relationships determining minimum project duration — zero or least total float. Typically 10-15% of activities are critical on a well-built schedule. The critical path shifts over the project life: early (foundations/structure), mid (MEP/enclosure), late (finishes/commissioning). Near-critical paths (within 10-20 working day threshold) are at risk of becoming critical.
Common Values: Critical: TF ≤ 0; Near-critical: TF 1-10 or 1-20 days per spec; Healthy schedule: 10-15% critical
Source Systems: P6 (Critical Path filter); MS Project; Procore
Procore Coverage: High — is_critical flag and total_float on activities enable critical path identification
Benchmarking Use: Critical path length and composition are key schedule characteristics
Notes: >40% near-critical means schedule is fragile — almost no recovery room. Critical path shift analysis reveals actual project progression vs. plan.

Description: Advanced techniques for tracing schedule logic beyond simple total float. Longest Path: traces driving relationships backward from project finish — more reliable than TF=0 when constraints distort float. Driving Relationships: the specific predecessor tie controlling each activity's early dates (not all predecessors drive). Path convergence: where multiple logic chains merge, increasing delay sensitivity.
Common Values: Longest path algorithm (P6), driving relationship identification, path convergence analysis
Source Systems: P6 (Longest Path filter); MS Project (limited); scheduling analysis tools
Procore Coverage: Low — not directly calculable from imported data; requires CPM tool analysis
Benchmarking Use: Path analysis reveals true schedule drivers vs. float-based critical path
Notes: P6's Longest Path algorithm is more accurate than TF=0 when constraints inflate/deflate float. MS Project has limited path analysis capability.

Description: Time-based earned value method — measures schedule performance in time units instead of cost units. ES = the time at which current earned value should have been achieved per baseline. SPI(t) = ES / Actual Time. Unlike traditional SPI (EV/PV) which converges to 1.0 at project end masking late delivery, ES-based SPI(t) remains accurate throughout. Growing adoption as the state-of-the-art schedule performance measurement.
Common Values: SPI(t) >1.0 = ahead; =1.0 = on schedule; <1.0 = behind; doesn't converge to 1.0 like traditional SPI
Source Systems: Calculated from P6/MS Project baseline + progress data; Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — requires baseline + progress + cost data combined; not natively calculated
Benchmarking Use: SPI(t) trend is the most accurate predictor of schedule outcome
Notes: Traditional cost-based SPI is unreliable for schedule measurement. ES requires disciplined baseline and progress tracking.

Category: --- SCHEDULE LIFECYCLE: baselines, updates, and deliverables ---

Description: The approved planned schedule against which all performance is measured — the contractual 'as-planned' reference. Original Baseline (BL0) submitted 30-90 days after NTP per typical specifications with 2-4 review cycles. Re-baselines (BL1, BL2) approved only for scope changes. What-if schedules test scenarios without affecting the live schedule. Baseline review checks specification compliance.
Common Values: BL0 = original, BL1/BL2 = re-baselines; submitted 30-90 days after NTP; 2-4 review cycles before approval
Source Systems: P6 (Maintain Baselines); MS Project (Set Baseline); Procore
Procore Coverage: Medium — baseline referenced via schedule metadata; parent schedule linkage connects baselines
Benchmarking Use: Time to baseline approval indicates preconstruction efficiency
Notes: BL0 is sacrosanct — modifications require formal owner approval. What-if analysis should always work on a copy, never the live schedule.

Description: The periodic progress recording and schedule recalculation cycle — the primary schedule management process. Monthly standard (bi-weekly on fast-track). Each update: advance data date, record actual dates, update remaining durations, adjust logic, recalculate CPM. Accompanied by a written narrative (2-10 pages) explaining what changed and why. Logic changes and out-of-sequence progress should be documented.
Common Values: Monthly (standard) or bi-weekly (fast-track); narrative 2-10 pages; Retained Logic preferred for out-of-sequence
Source Systems: P6; MS Project; Procore (schedule sync)
Procore Coverage: Medium — updated schedules imported from CPM tool; change tracking captures deltas between updates
Benchmarking Use: Update frequency and narrative quality indicate schedule management discipline
Notes: Out-of-sequence progress means field is working in different order than schedule logic. P6 offers Retained Logic (safer) vs. Progress Override (aggressive).

Description: Rolling short-term schedule showing daily/weekly detail for the next 3-6 weeks — the superintendent's primary planning tool. Three-week: daily detail for crew assignments, material deliveries, inspections. Six-week: weekly detail for coordination and procurement. Updated weekly in coordination meetings. More detailed than the CPM schedule — bridges the formal schedule to field execution.
Common Values: 3-week (daily detail, weekly update), 6-week (weekly detail, coordination focus)
Source Systems: P6 (filtered view); Excel; Procore (dedicated look-ahead entity with tasks, assignees, and company linkage)
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated look-ahead tables with tasks, assignees, and company linkage; first-class entity not just a filtered view
Benchmarking Use: Look-ahead adherence (planned vs. actual) measures short-term execution reliability
Notes: Look-aheads as a first-class entity with their own tasks and assignees is a differentiated capability vs. P6/MS Project filtered views.

Description: Plan to recover lost time and return to the contractual completion date after a delay. Required when negative float exceeds a contract threshold (often 10-20 days). Must show specific recovery actions: overtime, added crews, resequencing, prefabrication, additional shifts. Compressed durations without documented justification are not accepted by owners or courts.
Common Values: Triggered by negative float >10-20 days; methods: OT, added crews, resequencing, prefab, additional shifts
Source Systems: P6; MS Project; contract correspondence
Procore Coverage: Low — recovery schedules are CPM tool documents; not specifically modeled in project management platforms
Benchmarking Use: Recovery schedule submissions per project indicate schedule distress level
Notes: Must show specific recovery actions, not just compressed durations. A common contractual remedy when the project falls behind.

Description: Final schedule reflecting actual dates for all activities — the factual record of how construction actually progressed. All activities have actual start and actual finish with no remaining duration. Quality depends entirely on consistent monthly updating throughout the project. Required closeout deliverable and the primary evidence in delay disputes.
Common Values: All activities 100% complete with actual dates; no remaining duration; closeout deliverable
Source Systems: P6; MS Project; validated against daily logs and field records
Procore Coverage: Low — as-built is a CPM tool deliverable; field data from daily logs can validate actual dates
Benchmarking Use: As-built schedule accuracy enables post-project lessons learned and estimating improvement
Notes: If monthly updates were poor, the as-built is unreliable. Procore field data (daily logs, observations) can independently validate actual dates.

Category: --- RESOURCE & COST LOADING: resources and dollars tied to the schedule ---

Description: Trade-specific labor hours assigned to schedule activities — drives manpower histograms, workforce planning, and crew optimization. Shows when each trade peaks and valleys over the project. Essential for site logistics (parking, laydown, temporary facilities). Resource-loaded schedules connect the time model to the workforce — without them, the schedule has no resource constraints.
Common Values: Electrician-hours, carpenter-hours, ironworker-hours per activity; peak workforce timing
Source Systems: P6 (Resource Assignments); MS Project (Resource Sheet); validated against daily manpower logs
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily manpower logs capture actual labor; planned labor from imported schedule resource assignments
Benchmarking Use: Peak workforce and labor distribution by trade enable cross-project workforce planning
Notes: Daily manpower logs can validate planned vs. actual labor — a feedback loop standalone scheduling tools cannot provide.

Description: Major equipment utilization assigned to schedule activities — identifies mobilization timing, utilization gaps, and equipment-driven constraints. Tower crane is the most impactful — crane time allocation drives structural and enclosure sequences. Equipment moves between projects create inter-project constraints not visible within a single project schedule.
Common Values: Tower crane-days, pump truck-hours, excavator-days, hoist-hours per activity
Source Systems: P6 (Resource Assignments); equipment management software; daily equipment logs
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily equipment logs capture actual utilization; planned equipment from schedule resources
Benchmarking Use: Equipment utilization rate and crane time allocation efficiency
Notes: Tower crane availability is often the single biggest schedule constraint on high-rise projects.

Description: Dollar values distributed across schedule activities for earned value measurement and cash flow projection. Activity cost = labor + material + equipment + subcontract. Produces the S-curve (cumulative planned vs. actual cost over time) — the universal progress visualization. Early-loaded curves (front-loading pay applications) vs. realistic distribution is a common owner concern.
Common Values: Planned Value (PV), Earned Value (EV), Actual Cost (AC); monthly draw amounts; S-curve
Source Systems: P6 (Cost Accounts); MS Project (Cost fields); Excel; financial systems
Procore Coverage: Low — cost loading is a CPM tool function; financial data provides actual cost for EV comparison
Benchmarking Use: S-curve shape and EV variance reveal project health; cash flow accuracy indicates budget-schedule integration
Notes: Cost-loaded schedule connects the time model to the financial model. Misalignment between scheduled cost and actual billing is a warning sign.

Description: Adjusting activity timing within available float to smooth resource demand — avoid peaks and valleys without extending the project. Manual leveling (scheduler judgment) preferred in construction over auto-leveling (often produces impractical sequences). Uses free float to shift non-critical activities without affecting the critical path.
Common Values: Peak-to-average ratio; level within float only; manual preferred over auto-leveling
Source Systems: P6 (Level Resources); MS Project (Resource Leveling)
Procore Coverage: None — leveling is a CPM authoring function; not a field data activity
Benchmarking Use: Leveled vs. unleveled peak workforce indicates optimization potential
Notes: Auto-leveling in P6/MS Project can produce illogical sequences. Best practice: use float to manually smooth peaks. Never level the critical path.

Category: --- DELAY & CLAIMS: delay analysis, time extensions, and schedule disputes ---

Description: Categorization of delays by cause and remedy — determines entitlement to time extension and/or compensation. Excusable Compensable: owner-caused, time + money (design errors, late owner items). Excusable Non-Compensable: neither party at fault, time only (weather, force majeure). Non-Excusable: contractor-caused, no relief, may trigger liquidated damages. Concurrent: overlapping independent delays — most disputed. Pacing: intentional slowdown because another delay makes original pace unnecessary.
Common Values: Excusable Compensable (owner-caused), Excusable Non-Compensable (weather/force majeure), Non-Excusable (contractor), Concurrent (overlapping), Pacing (intentional slowdown)
Source Systems: P6 (analysis); daily logs; contract correspondence; RFIs; change events
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily delay logs capture delay events with type classification; change events link to time extensions
Benchmarking Use: Delay days by classification and responsibility drive time extension and claim negotiations
Notes: Concurrent delay has no universal rule — jurisdictions differ on apportionment. Procore daily delay logs capture weather and material delays with structured type field.

Description: The gold standard prospective delay analysis method — inserts a fragnet (delay event network of 3-20 activities) into the updated schedule at the time of impact and compares before/after CPM results. Requires: valid updated schedule at the time of delay, fragnet representing the delay event, and before/after comparison. Quality depends entirely on schedule update quality. Most accepted method in US construction.
Common Values: Fragnet: 3-20 activities representing the delay; before/after comparison shows critical path impact
Source Systems: P6 (primary tool); MS Project; expert analysis
Procore Coverage: Low — TIA is performed in the CPM tool; field data supports causation evidence
Benchmarking Use: TIA is the standard for quantifying individual delay event impact
Notes: A well-built fragnet accurately represents the delay event. Expert judgment required. Quality depends on having valid contemporaneous schedule updates.

Description: After-the-fact methods comparing planned vs. actual schedules. As-Planned vs. As-Built: simplest, compares BL0 to actual dates, shows variance but not causation. Collapsed As-Built (But-For): removes delay events from as-built to show what would have happened — favored by claimants but criticized as speculative. Windows Analysis: period-by-period analysis, most thorough and defensible but very labor-intensive.
Common Values: As-Planned vs. As-Built (simplest), Collapsed As-Built/But-For (claimant-favored), Windows Analysis (most thorough)
Source Systems: P6; expert analysis; schedule comparison tools
Procore Coverage: Low — retrospective analysis uses CPM tool data; change tracking provides supporting timestamps
Benchmarking Use: Method selection depends on schedule update quality and dispute complexity
Notes: Windows Analysis requires good schedule updates for each period. As-Planned vs. As-Built doesn't handle concurrent delay. Method selection is often contested.

Description: Formal written request for additional contract time due to an excusable delay event. Must be submitted within the contract notice period (typically 7-21 days). Requires: delay description, critical path impact demonstration, and supporting documentation. Timely notice is critical — many contracts bar late claims. Links to change events for cost impact when delay is also compensable.
Common Values: Submitted within 7-21 day notice period; includes schedule impact analysis and supporting documentation
Source Systems: Contract correspondence; P6 (TIA support); change management systems
Procore Coverage: Medium — change events capture time extension requests with schedule impact flags; RFIs flag schedule impact
Benchmarking Use: Time extension requests per project indicate delay exposure and contractual risk
Notes: Timely notice is critical — many contracts bar claims submitted after the notice period. Change events are the primary instrument for formal time extensions.

Description: Direction (explicit or constructive) to complete work faster than the adjusted contractual schedule. Explicit: written owner directive with cost reimbursement. Constructive: owner denies justified time extension but insists on original completion date, forcing contractor to accelerate at own cost — a major claim category. Methods: overtime, added crews, resequencing, prefabrication, additional shifts.
Common Values: Explicit (written directive with cost), Constructive (denied time extension + enforced date); Methods: OT, crews, prefab, shifts
Source Systems: Contract correspondence; schedule records; cost records (premium time, added resources)
Procore Coverage: Low — acceleration is a contractual/legal concept; field data and cost records capture underlying evidence
Benchmarking Use: Acceleration cost claims are among the largest schedule-related disputes
Notes: Constructive acceleration occurs when the owner denies a valid time extension but insists on the original date. Documentation of denial and resulting cost is critical.

Category: --- SCHEDULE QUALITY: specification compliance and health checks ---

Description: Defense Contract Management Agency schedule assessment framework — 14 checks covering logic, durations, float, constraints, relationships, resources, and critical path. Originally military but now the most widely recognized schedule health framework in commercial construction. Checks 1-9 universally applicable. Checks 10-14 require more mature scheduling. Passing 11+ out of 14 = healthy.
Common Values: 14 checks: open ends, leads, lags (<5%), relationship types (<5% non-FS), constraints (<5%), high float, negative float, high duration, invalid dates, resources, missed tasks, CP test, CPLI, BEI
Source Systems: P6 (Schedule Check); MS Project; scheduling analysis tools; partially calculable from imported data
Procore Coverage: Medium — several checks calculable from scheduling data (relationships/float/constraints); full assessment requires CPM tool
Benchmarking Use: DCMA compliance score is the industry standard for cross-project schedule quality comparison
Notes: Not all 14 points apply equally to every project. Commercial construction typically focuses on checks 1-9 and CPLI.

Description: Contract-specific schedule requirements that must be met for baseline approval and ongoing updates. Activity duration limits (typically 15-20 working day max), relationship type ratios (FS >80-90%), calendar assignment rules (cure on 7-day, work on 5/6-day), constraint limits (<5% non-ASAP), open end prohibitions. Non-compliant schedules are rejected in review.
Common Values: Max duration 15-20 working days; FS >80-90%; Constraints <5%; Open ends = 0; Calendar correct for activity type
Source Systems: P6 (Schedule Check); MS Project; specification review checklists
Procore Coverage: Medium — activity duration/relationship types/constraint types available for compliance checking
Benchmarking Use: Specification compliance rate across baseline submissions indicates scheduling maturity
Notes: Specs vary by owner and contract. Activities exceeding duration limits must be broken down. Wrong calendar assignment distorts durations.

Description: (Critical path remaining duration + total float) / critical path remaining duration — a single-number predictor of schedule outcome. CPLI > 1.0: buffer exists. CPLI = 1.0: no buffer. CPLI < 1.0: project will finish late without recovery. CPLI < 0.95 is a strong predictor of missing the completion date. Simple to calculate, powerful for executive reporting and trend analysis.
Common Values: CPLI >1.0 = buffer; =1.0 = no margin; <1.0 = projected late; <0.95 = high risk of missing completion
Source Systems: Calculated from P6/MS Project; calculable from imported scheduling data
Procore Coverage: Medium — calculable from total_float and activity duration data in scheduling tables
Benchmarking Use: CPLI is a single-number schedule health score for executive dashboards and cross-project comparison
Notes: One of the most powerful schedule metrics. Simple enough for non-schedulers to understand. Trend over time reveals schedule trajectory.

Category: --- SCHEDULE INTEGRATION: connecting the plan to the field ---

Description: The hierarchy of schedule detail — from executive summary to daily work plan. Master/Summary: 50-200 activities, phase milestones, the owner's view. Detailed CPM: 500-5000+ activities, full logic-driven network, the contractual schedule of record for performance measurement and delay analysis. Daily Work Plan: crew-level assignments for today's work. Each level should mathematically trace to the one above it.
Common Values: Master (50-200 activities), Detailed CPM (500-5000+), Daily Work Plan (crew-level)
Source Systems: P6 (detailed CPM); MS Project; Procore (imported CPM + daily scheduled work with task linkage)
Procore Coverage: High — detailed CPM imported via scheduling tables; daily work plans via scheduled work logs with FK to schedule tasks
Benchmarking Use: CPM activity count and daily plan completion rate indicate schedule management maturity
Notes: Disconnect between schedule levels is a common problem. Daily work plans that link to CPM tasks close the gap between planning and execution.

Description: Importing the detailed schedule from the authoring tool (P6, MS Project, Asta) into the project management platform. The schedule is authored externally and consumed broadly. Import via XER/XML/MPP format. Source activity ID maps to platform task ID for ongoing synchronization. Regular re-sync required after monthly updates.
Common Values: Import formats: XER (P6), XML, MPP (MS Project); sync frequency: monthly with updates
Source Systems: P6; MS Project; Asta Powerproject; Procore (P6/MS Project import); Autodesk Build; Oracle Aconex; InEight
Procore Coverage: High — P6 and MS Project import natively supported; activity ID mapping maintained across syncs
Benchmarking Use: Integration health = activity count and date agreement between authoring tool and project platform
Notes: Most GCs author schedules in P6 or MS Project but manage projects on a broader platform. Activity ID mapping is the bridge.

Description: Connecting CPM activities to field data — daily logs, inspections, RFIs — to validate progress and identify discrepancies between planned and actual execution. The gap between CPM and field reality is the central problem in construction scheduling. Most schedulers update from verbal reports. Systems connecting the planned schedule to daily logs provide independent validation — where project management platforms add value beyond standalone scheduling tools.
Common Values: Actual dates validated by daily logs; RFI schedule impact flags; manpower planned vs. actual; inspection completion linkage
Source Systems: Procore (native linkage via scheduled work FKs); P6 + field apps (manual reconciliation)
Procore Coverage: High — scheduled work logs link to schedule tasks via FK; daily logs capture planned vs. actual; RFIs flag schedule impact
Benchmarking Use: Schedule accuracy improves when validated against independent field data
Notes: P6 has no field data. Native field-to-schedule linkage is a differentiated capability for project management platforms vs. standalone scheduling tools.

Description: Timestamped record of every change to every schedule activity — capturing old values, new values, author, timestamp, and reason. Critical evidence in delay disputes: who changed what and when. Change frequency per activity identifies unstable areas. Change justification quality indicates scheduling discipline. Includes field-initiated schedule update requests where subcontractors propose changes for scheduler review.
Common Values: Old/new for start, finish, duration, percentage, name; reason/justification; author; timestamp
Source Systems: Procore (structured delta tracking); P6 Schedule Log (less structured); MS Project (compare versions)
Procore Coverage: High — structured change tracking with old/new values, timestamps, and reason field; unique audit capability
Benchmarking Use: Change frequency and reason distribution reveal schedule stability and management quality
Notes: Structured change tracking is more detailed than P6's Schedule Log. Field-initiated update requests add collaborative dimension. Reason field quality varies.

Category: --- LEAN & ALTERNATIVE SCHEDULING: methodologies beyond traditional CPM ---

Description: Collaborative scheduling methodology where the people doing the work make reliable promises. Pull Planning: trades work backward from milestones to identify handoffs and constraints. Make-Ready Planning: clearing constraints (materials, info, prerequisite work, space) before scheduling work. Percent Plan Complete (PPC): tasks completed as committed / tasks committed — the core reliability metric. Reasons for Non-Completion: root cause tracking of missed commitments.
Common Values: PPC >80% = healthy, >85% = world-class; Constraint types: prerequisite work, materials, equipment, labor, information, space
Source Systems: Touchplan; VPlanner; LeanTakt; Procore (scheduled work with showed/not-showed tracking; look-ahead tasks)
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily scheduled work logs capture plan-vs-actual with showed boolean (PPC equivalent); look-ahead tracks commitments
Benchmarking Use: PPC trend is the strongest predictor of reliable schedule execution; cross-trade PPC reveals bottleneck trades
Notes: PPC measures workflow reliability, not schedule progress. A project can be on schedule with low PPC (luck) or behind with high PPC (reliable but slow).

Description: Fixed-rhythm scheduling where each trade moves through zones at the same pace — manufacturing flow applied to construction. Takt time = available time / number of zones. All trades match the same rhythm. Train/wagon scheduling: trades move like train cars through zones in fixed sequence at fixed intervals. Exposes bottleneck trades immediately — if one trade can't match the takt, the whole flow stops.
Industry Standard: Lean Construction Institute; manufacturing lean principles
Common Values: Zone duration (takt) = available time / zones; all trades match same rhythm; 1-5 day takt typical
Source Systems: LeanTakt; VICO; custom spreadsheets; Procore (zone-based look-ahead)
Procore Coverage: Low — no native takt planning; zone-based scheduling can be approximated through look-ahead structure
Benchmarking Use: Zone throughput rate and takt adherence indicate production flow health
Notes: Growing adoption in repetitive construction (multi-family, hotels, hospitals). Reduces WIP and improves flow.

Description: Scheduling methods for repetitive or linear work where location is the primary axis. Line of Balance (LOB): graphical method showing production rate across identical units — each trade plotted as a line with slope = production rate. Time-Distance Diagram: for linear infrastructure (roads, tunnels, pipelines) with distance as the primary axis. Standard for repetitive work but not well-supported in traditional CPM tools.
Industry Standard: AACE; common in residential and infrastructure
Common Values: LOB: Y-axis=location, X-axis=time, slope=production rate; Time-Distance: X-axis=distance, Y-axis=time
Source Systems: Tilos (primary for linear); VICO; custom tools; P6 (limited)
Procore Coverage: None — linear scheduling requires specialized tools outside standard CPM import
Benchmarking Use: Production rate (units/day or LF/day) comparison reveals bottleneck trades across repetitive projects
Notes: P6 and MS Project cannot natively produce LOB or time-distance diagrams. Tilos is the industry standard for linear projects.

Description: Linking 3D BIM model elements to CPM schedule activities for visual construction sequence simulation (4D) and adding cost data for time-phased cost visualization (5D). Animation shows construction progression over time. Powerful for communicating sequence to non-schedule audiences. Requires maintained links between model, schedule, and cost — high effort but high communication value.
Common Values: 4D = model + schedule (sequence animation); 5D = model + schedule + cost (visual earned value)
Source Systems: Synchro (Bentley); Navisworks; Fuzor; VICO (Trimble); Procore BIM viewer (limited)
Procore Coverage: Low — BIM viewer supports model visualization but not native 4D/5D scheduling integration
Benchmarking Use: 4D model accuracy indicates coordination quality between schedule and design teams
Notes: Requires: accurate 3D model, maintained element-to-activity links, regular re-sync. 5D adds cost-loaded elements for visual earned value.

Category: --- STANDARD MEASURES: KPIs derived from schedule data ---

Description: Earned value / planned value — the primary schedule efficiency metric. Traditional SPI = EV/PV (cost-based, converges to 1.0 at project end, masking late delivery). Earned Schedule SPI(t) = ES/AT (time-based, remains accurate throughout). Calculation: SPI = cumulative EV / cumulative PV at data date. SPI(t) is the recommended variant for construction.
Common Values: SPI = 1.0 on schedule; <1.0 behind; >1.0 ahead; SPI(t) preferred for accuracy
Source Systems: Calculated from P6/MS Project; Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — requires baseline + progress + cost data combined; not natively calculated
Benchmarking Use: SPI trend across updates is a leading indicator of schedule outcome
Notes: Duration-based SPI is more meaningful for construction than cost-based. SPI(t) using Earned Schedule doesn't converge to 1.0.

Description: Activities completed on or before baseline finish date / total activities completed in the period — measures execution discipline. Simpler and more intuitive than SPI. Calculation: BEI = activities finishing ≤ BL finish / all activities finishing in period.
Common Values: BEI = 1.0 all on time; <0.8 significant slippage; >0.9 healthy execution
Source Systems: Calculated from P6/MS Project baseline comparison; calculable from imported scheduling data
Procore Coverage: Medium — calculable from scheduling data with baseline dates and actual finish dates
Benchmarking Use: BEI is easier to communicate than SPI; tracks execution discipline across projects
Notes: More intuitive than SPI for non-schedule audiences. Requires baseline dates. DCMA 14-Point check #14.

Description: Rate of total float decrease across schedule updates — reveals whether the schedule is tightening faster than planned work completion. Calculation: average TF change per update period or % of activities losing float period-over-period. Accelerating float erosion is a leading indicator of critical path expansion.
Common Values: Stable: avg TF change ≤ -2 days/update; Concerning: -5 to -10 days/update; Alarm: >-10 days/update
Source Systems: Calculated from P6/MS Project update comparison; calculable from scheduling change tracking data
Procore Coverage: Medium — float values tracked over time through schedule updates and change tracking
Benchmarking Use: Float erosion trend indicates whether the schedule is tightening faster than expected
Notes: Normal float erosion occurs as a project progresses. Abnormal erosion (faster than planned work completion) signals emerging risk.

Description: Milestones completed on or before their baseline date / total milestones due to date — a high-level schedule health KPI. Focuses on key events rather than all activities. More meaningful to owners and executives than SPI or float analysis.
Common Values: Healthy: >80%; Concerning: 60-80%; Alarm: <60%
Source Systems: Calculated from P6/MS Project + baseline; calculable from imported scheduling data
Procore Coverage: Medium — milestones identified by zero duration; baseline comparison available
Benchmarking Use: Milestone adherence is the executive-level schedule health metric
Notes: Contractual milestones vs. interim milestones should be tracked separately. Contractual misses may trigger liquidated damages.

Description: Percentage of remaining activities that are critical (TF ≤ 0) — measures how much of the schedule is at risk of delay. Calculation: critical activities / total remaining activities. Rising ratio indicates schedule deterioration as parallel paths converge and recovery room shrinks.
Common Values: Healthy: 10-15%; Concerning: >25%; Alarm: >40%
Source Systems: Calculated from P6/MS Project; calculable from is_critical flag and total_float
Procore Coverage: High — is_critical flag and total_float on activities enable direct calculation
Benchmarking Use: Critical ratio trend reveals whether the schedule is becoming more fragile over time
Notes: >40% critical means almost no recovery room. The ratio naturally increases toward project end.

Description: Percentage of weekly commitments completed as planned — the core lean scheduling metric from the Last Planner System. Calculation: PPC = tasks completed as committed / tasks committed. Measures workflow reliability, not schedule progress. A project can be on schedule with low PPC (luck) or behind with high PPC (reliable but slow).
Common Values: PPC >80% = healthy; >85% = world-class; <70% = workflow reliability problem
Source Systems: Touchplan; VPlanner; Procore (scheduled work showed/not-showed tracking)
Procore Coverage: High — daily scheduled work logs with showed boolean = PPC input; native plan-vs-actual tracking
Benchmarking Use: PPC trend is the strongest predictor of reliable execution; cross-trade PPC reveals bottleneck trades
Notes: Showed boolean on daily scheduled work is a direct PPC input. One of the few metrics capturing field execution reliability.

Description: Activities with progress that violates their logical predecessors / total in-progress activities — indicates schedule-to-field alignment. Calculation: out-of-sequence activities / total in-progress. High rate means the field is working in a different order than the schedule shows.
Common Values: Target: <5%; Concerning: >15%; indicates schedule logic doesn't match actual construction sequence
Source Systems: P6 (Retained Logic vs. Progress Override); MS Project
Procore Coverage: Low — requires CPM calculation logic; not directly calculable from imported schedule data
Benchmarking Use: Out-of-sequence rate indicates schedule-to-field alignment quality
Notes: P6 handles with Retained Logic (safer) or Progress Override (aggressive). Most specs require Retained Logic.

Description: Composite metric aggregating multiple quality indicators into a single score — typically based on DCMA 14-Point Assessment. Each check is binary pass/fail; total pass count produces the health score. Enables non-schedulers to understand schedule quality at a glance. Can be automated from imported schedule data for continuous monitoring.
Common Values: 14 checks, each pass/fail; 11+ passing = healthy; 8-10 = needs improvement; <8 = poor schedule
Source Systems: Calculated from P6/MS Project; partially calculable from imported scheduling data
Procore Coverage: Medium — partial data available (float/relationships/constraints/durations) for automated checking
Benchmarking Use: Schedule health score enables cross-project schedule quality comparison on a single scale
Notes: Automated health scoring from imported schedule data is a high-value analytics feature. Not all checks automatable from imported data.
